title: Five Generations on Finley Land
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine on September 13, 2011, Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan, born in Lincolnville in 1956, traces her fifth-generation roots to great-great-grandfather Thomas A. Finley, who came from the Carolinas with thirteen children and settled near Moultrie, and describes a segregated but self-sufficient Lincolnville of Black-owned grocery stores, beauty parlors, and the St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church her family helped found.

part: A Child in the Movement Years
title: Don't Look Back
summary: Duncan recalls being seven and a half when Dr. King came to St. Augustine, the Ku Klux Klan marching through Lincolnville at night while Black residents lined the streets, her mother holding her hand and telling her not to look back, classmates' homes being firebombed, her childhood confusion over Badcock Furniture as bad cops, and Dr. King crossing from Mr. Proctor's house to ask her mother if she would join the marches.

title: The Colored Sign Comes Down
summary: Venturing downtown around age ten to twelve, Duncan remembers being watched as a suspected thief in stores, the colored sign on the wall of the doctor's building near Flagler Hospital, and how, after the sign came down, she walked her younger sister straight down the once-forbidden front hallway only to be marched back through the office to the Black entrance at the rear.

title: Fighting Just to Eat Lunch
summary: Duncan describes her school closing after fifth grade at Excelsior Elementary and forced integration in sixth grade, teachers who would not teach her and children who would not sit by her, a junior high with no cafeteria where white students formed lines to block Black children from walking to Orange Street for lunch, and the violence she says made the schools feel anything but nonviolent.

title: Put On Your Thinking Cap
summary: Duncan tells how she learned the movement's history later in life through Catherine Twine and Cora Tyson Johnson, and how after Twine's death local historian David Nolan, with J.T. Johnson, Jane Billings, and others, asked her to help plan the fortieth anniversary; she proposed modeling it on a family reunion, recalling her own 2001 reunion and a cousin found in Madagascar who had been a Clinton-era ambassador.

title: Time to Say Thank You
summary: Duncan recounts the strategic meeting at the Galimore Center where older residents shared heart-wrenching stories, the decision to bring movement participants back to a history hidden by the city's power structures, a doubter who urged them to let sleeping dogs lie, J.T. Johnson's reply that it was time to say thank you, and her own grief that despite all Dr. Hayling and Dr. King did, much in St. Augustine, the first free Black settlement at Fort Mose nearby, had not changed.

title: The City He Loved
topic: A Son Who Could Not Stay
summary: Duncan speaks of stressing education with her five children and of her son, who earned an industrial and systems engineering degree from the University of Florida and a second degree in building construction yet could not find work in St. Augustine, and who wrote that the city he loved would never be the city he would return to, a pattern she says holds for many Black people there.

title: Angels Who Walked Among Us
summary: Duncan describes the all-volunteer 40th ACCORD, long unfunded until Northrop Grumman backed thirty historic civil rights markers placed with the permission of city officials and private citizens, and her wish to bring recognition to Dr. Robert Hayling, the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers, Audrey Nell Hamilton, and Rena Ayers, who at over a hundred had once housed and fed demonstrators and Northern attorneys, people she calls angels.

title: One Hand Wouldn't Cover It
summary: Asked what still has not changed, Duncan points to the scarcity of jobs and Black-owned businesses, so few one hand would cover them, the near-absence of Black people in St. Augustine and St. Johns County government and administration, and the flight of young people like her son, now in Jacksonville, and a daughter with a doctorate in physical therapy who is looking to leave.

title: Because It's the Money Maker
summary: Duncan attributes the city's new embrace of its civil rights history to tourist demand and the revenue it generates rather than moral transformation, crediting Andrew Young's film and the previewing of his movie for national exposure, noting the Foot Soldiers monument was a private venture the city credited only after the money was there, and that Northrop Grumman's funding contrasted with the unfriendly Fertile Hill company that preceded it.

title: A Paternalistic Little Town
summary: After a brief pause to change recording tracks, Duncan reflects that St. Augustine is a quiet, peaceful place to raise a family but, as Hank Thomas said, paternalistic, and argues that while the city can stay old and historic, the county must open to industry so that Black families, who have taken flight by the thousands, can come back and raise children as she once did in Lincolnville.

title: Toward a Civil Rights Museum
summary: Duncan outlines the 40th ACCORD's program: keynote speakers Dorothy Cotton and the coming Taylor Branch, freedom trail tours, and two Northrop Grumman-funded teacher workshops for St. Johns County built around a civil rights workbook, and the long-term goal, led by chair Richard Burton and co-chair J.T. Johnson, of opening a civil rights museum by 2014 to mark the fiftieth anniversary of the Civil Rights Act, before the closing thanks.
